text: The surgeon administers a perianal nerve block to the patient, aiming to reduce postoperative pain and relax the sphincter, which will visually result in a wider intersphincteric plane.
The surgeon identifies the intersphincteric plane and inserts a probe into the fistula tract. The seton is removed, and the intersphincteric plane is opened using diathermy.

The surgeon identifies the fistula tract and isolates it within the intersphincteric plane. A probe remains fixated in the tract as careful dissection encircles the tract, separating it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon removes the probe from the fistula tract and ligates the tract near the internal opening. The tract is then cut close to the internal opening.
The surgeon sutures the fistula tract at the site of the internal opening. The matured fistula tract is visible as a fibrotic tube.

The surgeon checks the internal opening of the fistula tract to confirm closure after suturing. A probe is inserted to locate the fistula tract at the site of the external opening, which is then sutured.
The surgeon checks the closure of the fistula tract, then closes the wound with sutures. The external opening is excised and corrected.
The surgeon excises the subcutaneous fistula tract, creating a fresh wound that extends to the level of the external sphincter. The fibrotic tube of the fistula tract is visible during this process.
The surgeon inserts paraffin gauze into the surgical site, completing the procedure.
